name: Cambodian Kuy Teav
steps: Simmer the pork in the broth until tender, about 20-25 minutes.
Cook the noodles separately, then combine with the broth, pork, and bean sprouts.
Serve hot.
steps_detailed: Simmer the pork with the garlic in the chicken broth for 20-25 minutes until tender, skimming any foam.
Stir in the fish sauce.
Cook the rice noodles separately according to package directions, drain, and place in a bowl.
Ladle the hot broth and pork over the noodles, top with bean sprouts and cilantro - Cambodia's beloved morning noodle soup, with roots in Teochew Chinese immigrant cooking.

name: Cambodian Rice Porridge (Bobor)
steps: Simmer the rice in the broth with the chicken and onion for 30-35 minutes, stirring occasionally, until thick and porridge-like.
Shred the chicken back into the porridge before serving.
steps_detailed: Simmer the rice, chicken, onion, and ginger in the broth for 32-40 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the rice has broken down into a thick, creamy porridge.
Remove the chicken, shred it, and stir back in along with the fish sauce.
Top with cilantro and serve hot - a comforting, everyday Cambodian breakfast.

name: Cambodian Fried Rice with Egg
steps: Fry the onion, then add the rice and stir-fry 3-4 minutes.
Push aside, scramble the egg, then mix everything together with the fish sauce.
steps_detailed: Fry the onion and garlic in the oil until soft and fragrant, 3-4 minutes.
Add the cooked rice and stir-fry 4-5 minutes, breaking up clumps.
Push the rice aside, scramble the egg in the cleared space, then mix everything together with the fish sauce.
A simple, everyday Cambodian breakfast made with leftover rice.

name: Cambodian Amok Trey
steps: Combine the fish with the coconut milk, egg, and turmeric.
Pour into a small dish and steam or bake at 350F for 25-30 minutes until set.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Cut the fish into large chunks and combine with the coconut milk, egg, turmeric, fish sauce, and garlic in a bowl.
Pour into a small buttered or oiled dish.
Steam over simmering water, or bake at 350F (175C) in a water bath, for 28-35 minutes until just set with a light custard-like texture.
Serve with steamed rice - amok is widely considered Cambodia's national dish, a fragrant steamed fish custard traditionally flavored with kroeung, a pounded herb paste.

name: Cambodian Green Mango Salad

ingredients.note: shredded, as a green mango stand-in

ingredients.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps: Toss the shredded cabbage with the lime juice and fish sauce.
Top with the crushed peanuts.
Serve chilled or at room temperature.

ingredients_full.note: shredded, as a green mango stand-in

ingredients_full.ingredient: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, with salt

steps_detailed: Toss the shredded cabbage (standing in for shredded unripe green mango) with the lime juice, fish sauce, and jalapeno.
Top with the crushed peanuts.
Let sit 10 minutes to let the flavors meld, then serve - a bright, tangy Cambodian salad, often paired with grilled meats or dried fish.

name: Cambodian Bai Sach Chrouk
steps: Marinate the pork with the garlic and sugar for 15-20 minutes.
Grill or pan-fry until well caramelized and cooked through, about 4-5 minutes per side.
Serve sliced over the rice.
steps_detailed: Marinate the thinly sliced pork with the garlic, sugar, fish sauce, and coconut milk for at least 20-30 minutes.
Grill over medium-high heat, or pan-fry, 4-6 minutes per side, until well caramelized on the edges and cooked through.
Slice and serve over the rice, traditionally with pickled vegetables and a light broth on the side - a beloved Cambodian breakfast-lunch dish, grilled pork and rice.

name: Cambodian Nom Banh Chok
steps: Poach the fish until cooked through, then flake it finely.
Combine the flaked fish with the coconut milk and simmer 5-6 minutes into a light sauce.
Serve over the noodles, topped with the shredded cabbage.
steps_detailed: Poach the fish with the garlic and turmeric until cooked through, 8-10 minutes, then flake it finely, discarding any bones.
Combine the flaked fish with the coconut milk and fish sauce, and simmer 6-8 minutes into a light, fragrant sauce.
Ladle over the rice noodles and top with the shredded cabbage and bean sprouts - often called Cambodia's national breakfast, this fish-based noodle dish is eaten across the country every morning.

name: Cambodian Lok Lak

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Marinate the beef with the garlic and fish sauce for 15-20 minutes.
Sear over high heat until browned but still tender, about 3-4 minutes per side.
Finish with the lime juice and serve with rice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Marinate the beef cubes with the garlic, half the fish sauce, and black pepper for 20-30 minutes.
Sear in the oil over high heat, 3-5 minutes per side, until well browned outside but still tender within.
Toss with the remaining fish sauce and lime juice off the heat.
Serve with rice and a simple lime-pepper dipping sauce - lok lak is one of Cambodia's most popular dishes, showing clear French colonial influence in its steak-like preparation.

name: Cambodian Samlor Korko
steps: Brown the pork with the onion.
Add the broth and green beans, and simmer 30-35 minutes until everything is tender.
Serve with rice.

ingredients_full.note: toasted and ground, as a thickener

steps_detailed: Toast the raw rice in a dry pan until golden, then grind into a coarse powder to use as a traditional thickener.
Brown the pork with the onion for 6-8 minutes.
Add the chicken broth, green beans, and fish sauce, and simmer 30-35 minutes until the pork and vegetables are tender.
Stir in the toasted rice powder to thicken the stew slightly.
Serve with rice - considered one of Cambodia's oldest and most treasured dishes, often described as its national soup.

name: Cambodian Grilled Chicken Skewers
steps: Marinate the chicken with the garlic, coconut milk, and turmeric for at least 20 minutes.
Thread onto skewers and grill until well charred and cooked through, about 10-12 minutes, turning occasionally.
Serve with rice.
steps_detailed: Marinate the cubed chicken with the garlic, coconut milk, turmeric, fish sauce, and sugar for at least 30 minutes, or overnight.
Thread onto skewers and grill over medium-high heat, turning occasionally, 12-15 minutes until well charred and cooked through.
Serve with rice and a peanut or cucumber dipping sauce - a popular Cambodian street food.

name: Cambodian Char Kroeung (Lemongrass Stir-Fry)
steps: Brown the beef with the garlic and ginger.
Stir in the fish sauce and cook 2-3 more minutes.
Serve over rice.
steps_detailed: Brown the beef in the oil with the garlic, ginger, and jalapeno over medium-high heat, breaking it up well, 8-10 minutes.
Stir in the fish sauce and cook 2-3 more minutes until fragrant.
Serve over rice - this stands in for the classic kroeung-based stir-fries built on a pounded paste of lemongrass, galangal, and turmeric root, foundational to Khmer cooking.

name: Cambodian Fried Spring Rolls (Cha Giò Style)
steps: Brown the chicken and mix with the cabbage.
Roll tightly in the wrapper, sealing with a little water.
Fry until golden and crisp, about 5-6 minutes.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground chicken with the onion, then mix with the shredded cabbage and fish sauce, cooking briefly.
Let cool slightly, then roll tightly in the wrapper, sealing the edge with a flour-water paste.
Fry in the oil over medium heat, 5-7 minutes, turning, until deeply golden and crisp - a popular snack shared across Cambodia and its neighbors.

name: Cambodian Num Kroch (Fried Banana Balls)
steps: Mix the mashed banana with the ground rice and sugar into a soft dough.
Shape into small balls and fry until golden, about 3-4 minutes.
steps_detailed: Grind the rice into a fine flour (or use pre-made sticky rice flour if available).
Mix the mashed banana with the rice flour and sugar into a soft, cohesive dough.
Shape into small balls and fry in the oil over medium heat, 3-5 minutes, turning, until golden.
Roll in shredded coconut while still warm and serve - a simple, sweet Cambodian street snack.

name: Cambodian Grilled Corn

ingredients.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps: Toss the drained corn kernels with the oil.
Pan-sear over medium-high heat, stirring occasionally, 8-10 minutes until charred in spots.
Finish with a squeeze of lime juice.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Corn, sweet, yellow, canned, drained solids, rinsed with tap water

steps_detailed: Toss the drained corn kernels with the oil.

Pan-sear in a hot, dry skillet or cast-iron pan, stirring occasionally, 8-12 minutes until charred in spots and heated through - this stands in for the classic whole grilled corn on the cob sold by street vendors.
Toss with a little fish sauce if using, and finish with a generous squeeze of lime juice - a common, simple roadside snack in Cambodia.

name: Cambodian Grilled Tofu Skewers
steps: Marinate the tofu with the garlic and fish sauce for 10-15 minutes.
Thread onto skewers and grill or pan-fry in the oil until golden, about 3-4 minutes per side.
steps_detailed: Press the tofu briefly to remove excess moisture, then cube it.
Marinate with the garlic, fish sauce, and sugar for 15-20 minutes.
Thread onto skewers and grill, or pan-fry in the oil, 3-5 minutes per side, until golden and slightly crisp at the edges.
A simple, popular vegetarian-friendly Cambodian snack.

name: Cambodian Sticky Rice with Coconut and Banana
steps: Warm the coconut milk with the sugar until dissolved.
Pour over the cooked rice and top with the sliced banana.
Serve warm.
steps_detailed: Warm the coconut milk with the sugar over low heat until the sugar dissolves, 2-3 minutes.
Pour most of the coconut mixture over the warm cooked rice, reserving a little.
Top with the sliced banana, the reserved coconut sauce, and the toasted coconut - a simple, beloved sweet found throughout Cambodia.

name: Cambodian Num Ansom (Sweet Rice Cake)
steps: Mix the cooked rice with the coconut milk, sugar, and cooked split peas.
Press into a small dish and steam for 20-25 minutes until firm.
Cool before slicing.
steps_detailed: Mix the cooked sticky rice thoroughly with the coconut milk, sugar, and cooked split peas (a stand-in for mung beans, traditionally used).
Press firmly into a small buttered dish or wrap in foil in place of banana leaf.
Steam over simmering water for 22-28 minutes until firm and set.
Cool before slicing - a traditional Cambodian sweet rice cake, often made for festivals.

name: Cambodian Sankhya (Pumpkin Custard)
steps: Whisk the eggs, coconut milk, and brown sugar together until smooth.
Pour into the hollowed sweet potato, or a small dish, and steam or bake at 325F for 30-35 minutes until set.
Cool before slicing.
steps_detailed: Whisk the eggs, coconut milk, brown sugar, and vanilla together until smooth and well combined.
Pour into the hollowed sweet potato (a stand-in for the small pumpkin traditionally used) or a small buttered dish.
Steam over simmering water, or bake in a water bath at 325F (165C), for 32-42 minutes until just set.
Cool before slicing into wedges - sankhya chek is a beloved Cambodian custard dessert, traditionally baked right inside a pumpkin.

name: Cambodian Kroeung-Style Lime Pepper Dip
steps: Whisk the lime juice, black pepper, and sugar together.
Serve alongside lok lak or grilled meats.
steps_detailed: Whisk the lime juice, black pepper, sugar, and garlic together until the sugar dissolves.
This simple lime-pepper sauce is the classic accompaniment to lok lak, letting the tang and heat balance the richness of the beef.

name: Cambodian Prahok-Style Dipping Sauce
steps: Brown the ground pork in a dry pan.
Mix with the fish sauce and jalapeno.
Serve as a bold, savory condiment alongside rice and vegetables.
steps_detailed: Brown the ground pork with the garlic in a dry pan until cooked through, 4-5 minutes.
Mix with the fish sauce and jalapeno.
This offers a milder nod to prahok, Cambodia's intensely pungent fermented fish paste that anchors much of the country's cooking - serve as a bold condiment alongside rice and fresh vegetables.
